text:
THE EALOI CANON
A Translation from the Original Languages
Preliminary Working Draft
Translated from the Westcott-Hort Greek text (NT material),
the Kirsopp Lake Greek text (Didache),
the Sahidic Coptic of Nag Hammadi Codex II,2 (Thomas),
and assorted critical editions for apocryphal fragments.
Preface
This is a working translation of the Jesus Tradition canon — a working set of source-language texts judged reliable as testimony to what Jesus said and did during his life and earthly presence (including resurrection, appearances, and ascension). It supersedes the earlier EALOI (Earliest Accessible Layer of Interpretation) canon. Where the EALOI selection used parsimony as its filter — the least-mediated source — this canon uses reliability for Jesus material specifically. Theological reflection about Jesus drops out; testimony to Jesus's words and deeds is what remains, even where the source is heavily mediated otherwise.
The translation prioritizes literal fidelity to source-language structure, lexical range, and historical-cultural context over conventional theological vocabulary. Strangeness in the Greek is allowed to produce strangeness in the English where the alternative would be domestication. Theologically loaded or polysemic terms are transliterated on first occurrence per pericope, with a proposed English rendering in brackets and a substantive endnote on first occurrence in the document.
Divine names are preserved in their original-language distinctions: Christos is rendered "Anointed," not "Christ"; anarthrous Kyrios in LXX citation is rendered "YHWH"; Kyrios as christological title for Jesus is rendered "Lord"; Kyrios as ordinary address is rendered contextually ("sir," "master"). Huios tou anthrōpou is rendered "the son of the human being." Theos is rendered "God" (anarthrous) or "the God" (with article, where Greek emphasis is present). Pater is rendered "Father." Where Jesus uses Abba (Mark 14:36), the Aramaic transliteration is preserved with the Greek translation following.
Translator's notes are placed as endnotes at the end of the document and numbered consecutively. A companion volume of working notes contains the standing glossary, decision log, and convention log; standing decisions documented there are applied throughout this translation.
The text-base for the canonical NT material is the Westcott-Hort 1881 critical edition; WH differs from the modern NA28/UBS5 in approximately 558 places (Aland's count), and significant divergences are flagged in the endnotes. The longer ending of Mark (16:9–20) is excluded; the original gospel ends at 16:8. For the Q material, both the Lukan and Matthean witnesses are translated, since Q is reconstructed and no manuscript exists; the translation generally treats the Lukan version as the primary witness (per Kloppenborg's methodology) and notes Matthew's parallel where it diverges substantively. For the Johannine material, only the pericopes treated as historical-narrative witness are included; the Johannine theological discourses, the Logos prologue, and the egō eimi discourses are excluded as Johannine theological composition rather than dominical speech. For the Pauline first-person appearance claims, the visionary-encounter caveat (per Galatians 1:16, en emoi) is preserved structurally — the form is given, but the category-difference from the synoptic appearance narratives is named in the endnotes. For the apocryphal material in Tier 3, each fragment is given a brief introductory note describing its date, source, and the scholarly judgment that brings it into the canon.
Tier 1: Primary Witnesses
These sources are reliable for Jesus material across their content (with redactional awareness for the gospels) and constitute the bulk of the canon.
A. The Gospel of Mark
kata Markon, c. 65–70 CE. The earliest narrative gospel. Translation ends at 16:8 with the original gospel; the so-called "longer ending" (16:9–20) is excluded as a 2nd-century addition.
Mark 1:1–8 — Forerunner pericope
[1]  Beginning of the euangelion [good announcement]1 of Jesus the Anointed.2 [2]  Just as has been written in Isaiah the prophet:3 "Behold, I am sending my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way"; [3]  "voice of one crying-out in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way of YHWH, make straight his paths'" —4 [4]  there came-to-be John, the Baptizing-one, in the wilderness, proclaiming a baptisma [immersion]5 of metanoia [turning]6 for aphesis [release]7 of hamartiai [failures].8 [5]  And there was going-out to him all the Judean countryside and all the Jerusalemites, and they were being immersed by him in the river Jordan, confessing-out their hamartiai. [6]  And John was clothed in camel-hair and a leather belt around his waist, and was eating locusts and wild honey.9 [7]  And he was proclaiming, saying, "He comes — the one stronger-than-me — after me; of whom I am not sufficient, stooping, to loose the strap of his sandals.10 [8]  I immersed you with water; but he will immerse you with holy pneuma [breath/spirit]."11
Mark 1:9–13 — Immersion and testing in the wilderness
[9]  And it came-to-be in those days, Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee and was immersed into the Jordan by John. [10]  And immediately, going-up out of the water, he saw the heavens being-torn-apart, and the pneuma like a dove descending into him.12 [11]  And a voice [came-to-be] out of the heavens: "You are my son, the beloved; in you I have-been-pleased." [12]  And immediately the pneuma drives him out into the wilderness. [13]  And he was in the wilderness forty days, being-tested by the Satan; and he was with the wild-beasts, and the messengers were attending him.13
Mark 1:14–15 — Inaugural proclamation
[14]  And after John had-been-handed-over,14 Jesus came into Galilee proclaiming the euangelion of God, [15]  [and saying] that "The kairos has been-fulfilled, and the basileia [reign]15 of God has drawn-near; turn, and pisteuete [trust]16 in the euangelion."
Mark 1:16–20 — Calling of the four
[16]  And passing alongside the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew the brother of Simon, casting nets in the sea — for they were fishers. [17]  And Jesus said to them, "Come behind me, and I will make you to become fishers of human-beings."17 [18]  And immediately, leaving the nets, they followed him. [19]  And going forward a little, he saw James the son of Zebedee and John his brother, and these in the boat mending the nets. [20]  And immediately he called them; and leaving their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired-workers, they went-off behind him.
Mark 1:21–28 — Synagogue exorcism in Capernaum
[21]  And they go-into Capernaum; and immediately, on the Sabbaths, going-into the synagogue, he was teaching. [22]  And they were astounded at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one having authority and not as the scribes. [23]  And immediately there was in their synagogue a human-being in an unclean pneuma, and he cried-out,18 [24]  saying, "What to us and to you, Jesus the Nazarene? Have you come to destroy us? I know you, who you are: the holy-one of God." [25]  And Jesus rebuked him [saying], "Be muzzled, and come-out from him." [26]  And the unclean pneuma, convulsing him and crying-out with a great voice, came-out from him. [27]  And all were amazed, so as to dispute with one another, saying, "What is this? A new teaching with authority — and he commands the unclean pneumata, and they obey him." [28]  And his fame went-out immediately everywhere into the whole surrounding-region of Galilee.
Mark 1:29–34 — Healings at the house of Simon
[29]  And immediately, going-out from the synagogue, they came into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John. [30]  And the mother-in-law of Simon was lying-down, fevered; and immediately they tell him about her. [31]  And approaching, he raised her, taking-hold of the hand; and the fever left her, and she was attending them. [32]  Now when evening came, when the sun set, they were bringing to him all those having-it-badly and the demonized. [33]  And the whole city was gathered together at the door. [34]  And he healed many having-it-badly with various diseases, and cast-out many demons; and he was not permitting the demons to speak, because they knew him [to be Anointed].19
Mark 1:35–39 — Solitary prayer
[35]  And early, while it was yet quite night, rising he went-out [and went-off] into a wilderness place, and there was praying. [36]  And Simon and those with him pursued him. [37]  And they found him, and they say to him that, "All are seeking you." [38]  And he says to them, "Let us go elsewhere into the neighboring towns, that I may also proclaim there — for I came-out for this." [39]  And he came proclaiming into their synagogues throughout the whole of Galilee, and casting-out the demons.
Mark 1:40–45 — Cleansing of a leper
[40]  And there comes to him a leper, exhorting him [and falling-on-his-knees], saying to him that, "If you will, you can cleanse me." [41]  And being-moved-in-the-bowels, stretching out his hand, he touched and says to him, "I will; be cleansed."20 [42]  And immediately the leprosy went-off from him, and he was cleansed. [43]  And growling at him, immediately he cast him out; [44]  and he says to him, "See that you say nothing to no-one, but go: show yourself to the priest, and offer concerning your cleansing what Moses commanded — for a witness to them." [45]  But going-out he began to proclaim much and to spread the word, so that he could no longer openly enter into a city, but was outside in wilderness places; and they were coming to him from every side.
Mark 2:1–12 — Paralytic and authority to release failures
[1]  And going-into Capernaum again, after some days it was heard that he was at home. [2]  And many were gathered, so that there was no longer room, not even at the door; and he was speaking to them the word. [3]  And they come, bringing to him a paralytic, being-carried by four. [4]  And not being-able to bring-him-near to him because of the crowd, they unroofed the roof where he was; and digging-out, they let-down the pallet on which the paralytic lay. [5]  And Jesus, seeing their pistis, says to the paralytic, "Child, your hamartiai are released."21 [6]  But there were some of the scribes sitting there, and reasoning in their kardiai [hearts]:22 [7]  "Why does this one speak in this way? He blasphemes! Who can release hamartiai except one — God?" [8]  And immediately Jesus, knowing in his pneuma that they were thus reasoning in themselves, says [to them], "Why do you reason these things in your hearts? [9]  What is easier: to say to the paralytic, 'Your hamartiai are released,' or to say, 'Rise [and] take up your pallet and walk'? [10]  But that you may know that the son of the human-being has authority on the earth to release hamartiai" — he says to the paralytic —23 [11]  "To you I say, rise, take up your pallet, and go to your house." [12]  And he was raised, and immediately taking-up the pallet went-out before all, so that all were amazed and were glorifying God [saying] that, "We have never seen anything like this."
Mark 2:13–17 — Calling of Levi; eating with the failed
[13]  And he went-out again alongside the sea, and all the crowd was coming to him, and he was teaching them. [14]  And passing-alongside, he saw Levi the son of Alphaeus seated at the toll-booth, and he says to him, "Follow me." And rising, he followed him. [15]  And it comes-to-be: he was reclining-at-table in his house, and many tax-collectors and failed-ones were reclining-with Jesus and his disciples — for there were many, and they were following him.24 [16]  And the scribes of the Pharisees, seeing that he was eating with the failed-ones and tax-collectors, were saying to his disciples that, "He eats with the tax-collectors and failed-ones?" [17]  And hearing, Jesus says to them that, "Those who are strong have no need of a physician, but those who have-it-badly. I came not to call dikaious [just-ones]25 but failed-ones."
Mark 2:18–22 — Question about fasting; new wine
[18]  And the disciples of John and the Pharisees were fasting; and they come and say to him, "Why do the disciples of John and the disciples of the Pharisees fast, but your [disciples] do not fast?" [19]  And Jesus said to them, "Are the sons of the bridal-chamber able to fast while the bridegroom is with them? As long as they have the bridegroom with them, they cannot fast. [20]  But days will come when the bridegroom is taken-away from them, and then they will fast in that day. [21]  No-one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment; otherwise the new fullness pulls-away from it — the new from the old — and the tear becomes worse. [22]  And no-one casts new wine into old wineskins; otherwise the wine will burst the skins, and the wine is destroyed and the skins. [But new wine into new skins.]"
Mark 2:23–28 — Sabbath grain-plucking
[23]  And it came-to-be on the Sabbaths he was passing-through the grain-fields, and his disciples began to make a way, plucking the heads-of-grain. [24]  And the Pharisees were saying to him, "Look — why do they do on the Sabbaths what is not lawful?" [25]  And he says to them, "Have you never read what David did when he had need and was hungry, he and those with him? [26]  How he went into the house of God in the time of Abiathar the chief-priest, and ate the bread of the presentation — which it is not lawful to eat except for the priests — and gave also to those with him?"26 [27]  And he was saying to them, "The Sabbath came-to-be on account of the human-being, and not the human-being on account of the Sabbath. [28]  So that the son of the human-being is Lord even of the Sabbath."
Mark 3:1–6 — Healing of the withered hand
[1]  And he went-into the synagogue again; and there was a human-being there having a withered hand. [2]  And they were watching him to see whether he would heal him on the Sabbaths, in order to accuse him. [3]  And he says to the human-being having the withered hand, "Rise into the middle." [4]  And he says to them, "Is it lawful on the Sabbaths to do good or to do harm — to psychēn [life/self]27 save or to kill?" But they were silent. [5]  And looking around at them with anger, grieved at the hardness of their heart, he says to the human-being, "Stretch out your hand." And he stretched it out, and his hand was restored. [6]  And the Pharisees, going-out, immediately took counsel with the Herodians against him, in order that they might destroy him.
Mark 3:7–12 — Crowds beside the sea
[7]  And Jesus, with his disciples, withdrew toward the sea; and a great multitude from Galilee followed; and from Judea, [8]  and from Jerusalem, and from Idumea, and beyond the Jordan, and around Tyre and Sidon, a great multitude, hearing what he was doing, came to him. [9]  And he told his disciples that a small-boat should keep-ready for him because of the crowd, that they not press him. [10]  For he had healed many, so that all who had afflictions fell-upon him to touch him. [11]  And the unclean pneumata, whenever they saw him, fell-down before him and cried-out, saying that, "You are the son of God." [12]  And he was rebuking them severely, that they should not make him manifest.
Mark 3:13–19 — Appointment of the Twelve
[13]  And he goes-up into the mountain, and calls-to-himself those whom he willed; and they went-off to him. [14]  And he made twelve, whom he also named apostolous [sent-ones],28 that they might be with him, and that he might send them to proclaim, [15]  and to have authority to cast-out the demons. [16]  And he made the twelve, and put on Simon a name, Peter; [17]  and James the son of Zebedee, and John the brother of James, and put on them a name Boanerges, which is sons of thunder; [18]  and Andrew, and Philip, and Bartholomew, and Matthew, and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus, and Simon the Cananaean, [19]  and Judas Iscarioth, who also handed him over.
Mark 3:20–35 — Beelzebul controversy and true family
[20]  And he comes into a house, and the crowd comes-together again, so that they could not even eat bread. [21]  And those-from-him, hearing, came-out to seize him, for they were saying that he was beside-himself.29 [22]  And the scribes, the ones from Jerusalem coming-down, were saying that he had Beelzebul, and that by the ruler of the demons he was casting-out the demons. [23]  And calling them to himself, in parables he was saying to them, "How can Satan cast-out Satan? [24]  And if a basileia is divided against itself, that basileia cannot stand. [25]  And if a household is divided against itself, that household will not be able to stand. [26]  And if Satan rose against himself and was divided, he cannot stand, but has an end. [27]  But no-one can enter into the house of the strong-one and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong-one; and then he will plunder his house.30 [28]  Amen, I say to you that all things will be released to the sons of human-beings — the failure-acts and the blasphemies, whatsoever they may blaspheme; [29]  but whoever blasphemes against the holy pneuma has no release into the aiōn but is liable for an aiōnion [of-the-age]31 failure-act" — [30]  because they were saying, "He has an unclean pneuma." [31]  And his mother and his brothers come, and standing outside they sent to him, calling him. [32]  And a crowd was sitting around him, and they say to him, "Behold, your mother and your brothers [and your sisters] outside are seeking you." [33]  And answering them, he says, "Who is my mother and my brothers?" [34]  And looking around at those sitting in a circle around him, he says, "Behold, my mother and my brothers! [35]  Whoever does the will of God — this one is my brother, and sister, and mother."
Mark 4:1–9 — The sower
[1]  And again he began to teach alongside the sea; and a very great crowd gathers to him, so that he, embarking into a boat, sat in the sea; and the whole crowd was upon the land at the sea. [2]  And he was teaching them many things in parables, and was saying to them in his teaching: [3]  "Listen — behold, the sower went-out to sow. [4]  And it came-to-be in the sowing, some fell alongside the way, and the birds came and ate it down. [5]  And other fell on the rocky-ground [and] where it had not much earth, and immediately it sprang-up, through the not having depth of earth. [6]  And when the sun rose, it was scorched, and through the not having root it withered. [7]  And other fell into the thorns, and the thorns came-up and choked it, and it gave no fruit. [8]  And other fell into the good earth, and was giving fruit, coming-up and growing, and it bore unto thirty and unto sixty and unto a hundred." [9]  And he was saying, "Whoever has ears to hear, let him hear."
Mark 4:10–20 — Why parables; the sower explained
[10]  And when he came-to-be alone, those around him with the twelve were asking him about the parables. [11]  And he was saying to them, "To you the mystērion of the basileia of God has been-given; but to those outside, the all-things come-to-be in parables,32 [12]  in order that 'seeing they may see and not perceive, and hearing they may hear and not understand, lest they turn-back and it be released to them.'"33 [13]  And he says to them, "Do you not know this parable? And how will you know all the parables? [14]  The sower sows the logos. [15]  And these are the ones alongside the way where the logos is sown: when they hear, immediately the Satan comes and takes-up the logos sown into them. [16]  And these are likewise the ones sown on the rocky-ground: who, when they hear the logos, immediately receive it with joy, [17]  and they have no root in themselves but are temporary; then, oppression or persecution coming-to-be on account of the logos, immediately they are scandalized. [18]  And others are those sown into the thorns; these are the ones who hear the logos, [19]  and the anxieties of the aiōn and the deceit of riches and the desires for the rest, entering-in, choke the logos together, and it becomes fruitless. [20]  And those are the ones sown on the good earth, who hear the logos and accept and bear-fruit unto thirty and [unto] sixty and [unto] a hundred."
Mark 4:21–25 — Lamp; measure
[21]  And he was saying to them that, "Does the lamp come, in order that it may be put under the modion-measure or under the bed? Not, that it may be put on the lamp-stand? [22]  For nothing is hidden, except that it may be made-manifest; nor came it to be secret, except that it may come into the manifest. [23]  If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear." [24]  And he was saying to them, "See what you hear: in what measure you measure, it shall be measured to you, and shall be added to you. [25]  For whoever has, to him shall be given; and whoever has not, even what he has shall be taken from him."
Mark 4:26–29 — Seed growing secretly
[26]  And he was saying, "Thus is the basileia of God: as a human-being should cast the seed upon the earth, [27]  and should sleep and rise night and day, and the seed sprouts and lengthens — how, he himself does not know. [28]  Of-itself the earth bears-fruit, first blade, then ear, then full grain in the ear. [29]  And whenever the fruit yields itself, immediately he sends-forth the sickle, because the harvest has-come."
Mark 4:30–34 — Mustard seed
[30]  And he was saying, "How shall we liken the basileia of God, or in what parable shall we set it? [31]  As a grain of mustard, which when it is sown upon the earth is smaller than all the seeds upon the earth, [32]  and when it is sown comes-up and becomes greater than all the herbs, and makes great branches, so that under its shade the birds of the heaven are able to nest." [33]  And with many such parables he was speaking the logos to them, just as they were able to hear; [34]  and apart from a parable he was not speaking to them; but privately to his own disciples he was loosening all things.
Mark 4:35–41 — Stilling of the sea
[35]  And he says to them on that day, evening having come, "Let us go-through to the other side." [36]  And leaving the crowd, they take him along, just-as he was, in the boat; and other boats were with him. [37]  And there comes-to-be a great storm of wind, and the waves were beating into the boat, so that the boat was already being filled. [38]  And he was in the stern, asleep on the cushion; and they wake him and say to him, "Teacher, does it not concern you that we are perishing?" [39]  And being-roused he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, "Be silent, be muzzled." And the wind ceased, and there came-to-be a great calm. [40]  And he said to them, "Why are you so cowardly? Have you not yet pistis?" [41]  And they feared a great fear, and were saying to one another, "Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"
Mark 5:1–20 — Gerasene demoniac
[1]  And they came to the other side of the sea, into the country of the Gerasenes. [2]  And he going-out from the boat, immediately there met him out from the tombs a human-being in an unclean pneuma, [3]  who had his dwelling in the tombs; and not even with a chain could anyone any longer bind him, [4]  through his having been many times bound with shackles and chains, and the chains having been pulled-apart by him and the shackles having been broken to pieces; and no-one had strength to subdue him. [5]  And throughout, night and day, in the tombs and in the mountains he was crying-out and cutting himself with stones. [6]  And seeing Jesus from afar, he ran and fell-down-before him, [7]  and crying-out with a great voice he says, "What to me and to you, Jesus, son of the most-high God? I adjure you by God, do not torment me." [8]  For he was saying to him, "Come-out, the unclean pneuma, from the human-being." [9]  And he was asking him, "What is your name?" And he says to him, "Legion is my name, because we are many." [10]  And he was exhorting him much that he not send them outside the country. [11]  Now there was there toward the mountain a great herd of swine, feeding. [12]  And they exhorted him saying, "Send us into the swine, that into them we may enter." [13]  And he permitted them; and going-out, the unclean pneumata entered into the swine, and the herd rushed down the cliff into the sea — about two thousand — and they were drowning in the sea. [14]  And those feeding them fled and reported into the city and into the fields; and they came to see what it is, the having-happened. [15]  And they come to Jesus and observe the demonized one sitting clothed and sound-of-mind — the one having had the legion — and they were afraid. [16]  And those-having-seen reported to them how it came-to-be to the demonized-one, and concerning the swine. [17]  And they began to exhort him to depart from their borders. [18]  And as he was embarking into the boat, the one having-been-demonized was exhorting him that he might be with him. [19]  And he did not permit him, but says to him, "Go to your house, to your own, and report to them what the Lord has done for you, and that he had-mercy on you." [20]  And he went-off and began to proclaim in the Decapolis what Jesus had done for him; and all were marveling.
Mark 5:21–43 — Jairus's daughter and the woman with the flow of blood
[21]  And Jesus having crossed-over [in the boat] again to the other side, a great crowd gathered to him, and he was alongside the sea. [22]  And there comes one of the rulers of the synagogue, named Jairus; and seeing him, he falls at his feet, [23]  and exhorts him much, saying that, "My little-daughter is at the last; that coming you may put your hands on her, that she may be saved and may live." [24]  And he went-off with him; and a great crowd was following him, and they were pressing him. [25]  And a woman being in a flow of blood twelve years, [26]  and having suffered much under many physicians, and having spent everything from her, and having been benefited nothing but rather having come into the worse, [27]  hearing the things about Jesus, having come in the crowd from-behind, touched his garment. [28]  For she was saying that, "If I touch even his garments, I shall be saved." [29]  And immediately the source of her blood was dried-up, and she knew in the body that she had-been-healed from the affliction. [30]  And immediately Jesus, having known in himself the power having-gone-out from him, having turned in the crowd was saying, "Who touched my garments?" [31]  And his disciples were saying to him, "You see the crowd pressing you, and you say, 'Who touched me?'" [32]  And he was looking-around to see the one having done this. [33]  And the woman, having-been-afraid and trembling, knowing what had-happened to her, came and fell-before him and told him all the truth. [34]  And he said to her, "Daughter, your pistis has saved you; go into peace, and be healthy from your affliction."34 [35]  While he was yet speaking, they come from the synagogue-ruler saying that, "Your daughter has died; why still trouble the teacher?" [36]  But Jesus, overhearing the logos being spoken, says to the synagogue-ruler, "Do not fear, only trust." [37]  And he did not permit anyone to follow with him except Peter and James and John the brother of James. [38]  And they come into the house of the synagogue-ruler, and he observes a tumult and people weeping and wailing much; [39]  and going-in he says to them, "Why are you tumult-ing and weeping? The child has not died, but is sleeping." [40]  And they were laughing-down at him. But he, casting all out, takes-along the father of the child and the mother and those with him, and goes-in where the child was. [41]  And taking-hold of the hand of the child, he says to her, "Talitha koum," which is, being-translated, "Little-girl, to you I say, rise."35 [42]  And immediately the little-girl rose-up and was walking-about; for she was twelve years old. And immediately they were beside-themselves with great amazement. [43]  And he charged them much that no-one should know this, and said to give her to eat.
Mark 6:1–6 — Rejection at Nazareth
[1]  And he went-out from there, and comes into his fatherland, and his disciples follow him. [2]  And the Sabbath having come, he began to teach in the synagogue; and the many hearing were astounded, saying, "Whence to this one these things? And what is this wisdom given to him? And such mighty-works coming-to-be through his hands? [3]  Is not this one the artisan, the son of Mary and brother of James and Joses and Judas and Simon? And are not his sisters here with us?" And they were being scandalized in him. [4]  And Jesus was saying to them that, "A prophet is not without-honor except in his fatherland and among his relatives and in his house." [5]  And he could not do there even one mighty-work, except that laying his hands on a few sick-ones he healed. [6]  And he was marveling at their lack-of-pistis. And he was going-around the villages in a circle, teaching.

B. Q — The Sayings Source (Jesus material only)
c. 40s–50s CE, reconstructed. For each Q passage, the Lukan version is treated as the primary witness (per Kloppenborg's methodology); Matthew's parallel is given when it diverges substantively. No manuscript of Q exists; what is presented here is Claude's translation of the WH Lukan text (with WH Matthean text where parallel translation is meaningful), not a translation of any reconstructed Q. Note on Q numbering: the original Q1 of the EALOI canon (Luke 3:7–9, 16–17 // Matthew 3:7–12) is John the Baptist's preaching — speech of John, not of Jesus — and falls outside the new canon's reliability-for-Jesus-material criterion. Following passages retain their conventional Q numbering (Q2, Q3, …) for cross-reference convenience with the prior canon and with Kloppenborg's Excavating Q.

C. Special L (Luke-only material)
Material found only in the Gospel of Luke (Westcott-Hort Greek text). Selected on the criterion of likely pre-Lukan tradition rather than Lukan composition; the most influential and most plausibly traditional units are included. The Magnificat (Luke 1:46–55) is excluded as Mary's speech rather than Jesus's, falling outside the Jesus-tradition criterion.

[4]  And he was not-willing for a chronos; but after these things, he said in himself, 'Even-if the God I do not fear nor a human-being I respect, [5]  yet on-account-of the supplying me toil this widow, I shall avenge her, lest into [the] end coming she give-me-a-black-eye.'" [6]  And the Kyrios said, "Hear what the judge of the injustice says. [7]  And shall not the God do the avenging of his chosen-ones, the crying-out to him day and night, and is he long-suffering over them? [8]  I say to you that he shall do the avenging of them quickly. Nevertheless, the son of the human-being having-come, then shall he find the pistis on the earth?"
Luke 18:9–14 — Pharisee and tax-collector
[9]  And he said also to certain of those having-trusted on themselves that they are just, and despising the rest, this parable: [10]  "Two human-beings went-up into the Temple to pray — the one a Pharisee, and the other a tax-collector. [11]  The Pharisee, having-stood, these things to himself was-praying: 'God, I-give-thanks to you, that I am not as the rest of the human-beings — robbers, unjust-ones, adulterers — or even as this tax-collector. [12]  I fast twice of the Sabbath, I tithe all-things whatever I acquire.' [13]  But the tax-collector, from afar standing, was not wishing not even the eyes to lift-up to the heaven, but was-beating his breast saying, 'God, be-propitious to me the failed-one.' [14]  I say to you, this one went-down justified to his house — rather than that one. For every-one exalting himself shall be humbled, and the one humbling himself shall be exalted."
Luke 19:1–10 — Zacchaeus
[1]  And entering, he was-going-through the Jericho. [2]  And behold, a man being-called by name Zacchaeus, and he was a chief-tax-collector, and he was rich. [3]  And he was-seeking to see the Jesus, who he is, and could not from the crowd, because in stature small he was. [4]  And running-up to the front, he went-up upon a sycamore-fig, in order that he might see him; for [by] there he was about to pass-by. [5]  And as he came at the place, looking-up, [the] Jesus said to him, "Zacchaeus, hurrying come-down; for today in your house I-must remain." [6]  And hurrying he came-down, and received him rejoicing. [7]  And seeing, all were-grumbling, saying that, "With a failed-one human-being he entered to lodge." [8]  And standing, Zacchaeus said to the Kyrios, "Behold, the half of my belongings, Kyrie, to the poor I give; and if-of-anyone anything I extorted-by-false-accusation, I am-giving-back four-fold." [9]  And [the] Jesus said to him that, "Today salvation came-to-be in this house, on-the-account that also he is a son of Abraham. [10]  For the son of the human-being came to seek and to save the having-been-lost."
Luke 23:34a — Word from the cross (textually disputed)
[34a]  [And [the] Jesus was saying, "Father, release to them; for they do not know what they do."]60
Luke 23:39–43 — Promise to the criminal
[39]  And one of the having-been-hanged criminals was-blaspheming him: "Are you not the Anointed? Save yourself and us." [40]  And answering, the other rebuking him said, "Do you not even fear the God, that under the same condemnation you are? [41]  And we indeed justly — for things-worthy of what we did we are receiving-back; but this-one nothing out-of-place did." [42]  And he was saying, "Jesus, remember me whenever you may come into your basileia." [43]  And he said to him, "Amen, to you I say, today with me you shall be in the paradise."
D. Special M (Matthew-only material)
Material found only in the Gospel of Matthew. Four representative parables peculiar to Matthew are translated.
Matthew 13:44–46 — Treasure and pearl
[44]  "The basileia of the heavens is like a treasure having-been-hidden in the field, which a human-being finding hid; and from the joy of him he goes and sells all things which-as he has, and buys that field. [45]  Again, the basileia of the heavens is like a human-being a merchant seeking good pearls; [46]  and finding one of-much-value pearl, going-off he has-sold all things, as-many-as he had, and bought it."
Matthew 18:23–35 — Unforgiving servant
[23]  "On-account-of this the basileia of the heavens has-become-like a human-being, a king, who wished to settle a logos with his slaves. [24]  And as he began the to-settle, there was-brought-near to him one debtor of ten-thousand talents. [25]  And he not having to give-back, the lord ordered him to be-sold and the wife and the children and all-things, as-many-as he had, and to be-given-back. [26]  Falling therefore, the slave was-doing-obeisance to him saying, 'Be-patient with me, and all things to you I shall give-back.' [27]  And being-moved-in-the-bowels, the lord of that slave released him, and the loan he forgave him. [28]  But going-out that slave found one of his fellow-slaves, who was-owing him a hundred denarii; and seizing him he was-choking [him], saying, 'Give-back if-anything you owe.' [29]  Falling therefore, his fellow-slave was-exhorting him, saying, 'Be-patient with me, and I shall give-back to you.' [30]  But he was not willing; rather, going-off he cast him into prison until he should give-back the being-owed. [31]  Seeing therefore his fellow-slaves the things having-come-to-be, they were-grieved exceedingly, and going-off they-related to their lord all the things having-come-to-be. [32]  Then calling-to-himself him, his lord says to him, 'Evil slave, all that debt I forgave you, since you exhorted me; [33]  was it not necessary also for you to have-mercy on your fellow-slave, as I also you had-mercy?' [34]  And being-angered, his lord handed him over to the torturers until he should give-back all the being-owed. [35]  Thus also my Father, the heavenly, shall do to you, if you do not release each-one to his brother from your hearts."
Matthew 20:1–16 — Workers in the vineyard
[1]  "For like is the basileia of the heavens to a human-being, a householder, who went-out at-dawn to hire workers for his vineyard. [2]  And having-agreed with the workers from a denarius for the day, he sent them into his vineyard. [3]  And going-out about the third hour, he saw others standing in the marketplace idle. [4]  And to those-ones he said, 'Go also you into the vineyard, and what may be just I shall give to you.' [5]  And they went-off. Again going-out about the sixth and ninth hour, he did likewise. [6]  And about the eleventh going-out he found others standing, and says to them, 'Why here have-you-stood the whole day idle?' [7]  They say to him, 'Because no-one us has-hired.' He says to them, 'Go also you into the vineyard.' [8]  And evening having-come, the lord of the vineyard says to his steward, 'Call the workers and give-back to them the wage, beginning from the last unto the first.' [9]  And coming, the about the eleventh hour, they received each a denarius. [10]  And coming, the first supposed that more they shall receive; and they received each a denarius — also they. [11]  And receiving, they were-grumbling against the householder [12]  saying, 'These last-ones one hour have-done, and equal to us them you have-made, the having-borne the burden of the day and the heat.' [13]  And answering one of them, he said, 'Friend, I do not wrong you; not from a denarius did you agree with me? [14]  Take what is yours, and go; and I wish to this last give as also to you. [15]  [Or] is it not lawful for me what I will to do with my-things? Or is your eye evil that I am good?' [16]  So shall be the last first and the first last."
Matthew 25:31–46 — Sheep and goats
[31]  "And whenever shall come the son of the human-being in his doxa, and all the messengers with him, then he shall sit upon a throne of his doxa; [32]  and there shall be gathered before him all the gentiles, and he shall separate them from one-another, just-as the shepherd separates the sheep from the goats; [33]  and he shall set the sheep at his right, and the goats at the left. [34]  Then shall say the king to those at his right, 'Come, the having-been-blessed of my Father, inherit the having-been-prepared for you basileia from foundation of kosmos. [35]  For I-hungered and you gave me to eat; I-thirsted and you gave-me-drink; a stranger I was and you gathered-me-in; [36]  naked, and you clothed me; I-was-weakened and you visited me; in prison I was and you came to me.' [37]  Then shall answer to him the just-ones saying, 'Kyrie, when did we see you hungering and feed, or thirsting and give-to-drink? [38]  And when did we see you a stranger and gather-in, or naked and clothe? [39]  And when did we see you weakened or in prison, and come to you?' [40]  And answering, the king shall say to them, 'Amen I say to you, in-as-much-as you did to one of these of my brothers the least-ones, to me you did.' [41]  Then shall he say also to those on the left, 'Go from me, the having-been-cursed, into the fire the of-the-age the having-been-prepared for the Slanderer and his messengers. [42]  For I-hungered and you did not give me to eat; I-thirsted and you did not give-me-drink; [43]  a stranger I was and you did not gather-me-in; naked, and you did not clothe me; weakened and in prison, and you did not visit me.' [44]  Then shall answer also they saying, 'Kyrie, when did we see you hungering or thirsting or a stranger or naked or weakened or in prison, and did not serve you?' [45]  Then shall he answer them saying, 'Amen I say to you, in-as-much-as you did not [do] to one of these the least-ones, neither to me did-you-[do].' [46]  And these shall go-off into chastisement aiōnion; but the just-ones into zōēn aiōnion."
E. The Gospel of Thomas (ⲡⲉⲩⲁⲅⲅⲉⲗⲓⲟⲛ ⲡⲕⲁⲧⲁ ⲑⲱⲙⲁⲥ) — Selected Sayings
Sahidic Coptic, Nag Hammadi Codex II,2, c. 350 CE manuscript (translation of an originally Greek collection, likely 1st-century in its earliest layer). Greek fragments preserved in P.Oxy 1, 654, and 655 are cited where they parallel the Coptic. Standard editions: Layton 1989 (Nag Hammadi Codex II,2–7, NHS XX); Plisch 2008.
The included sayings are those satisfying the corroboration criterion: a logion is included as a unit (whole-logion principle) when independent attestation in early Christian or Jewish streams supports any portion of it, and when no clear borrowing from non-Christian or non-Jewish ascetic-philosophical traditions overwhelms the corroborative material.
Coptic-specific conventions: nomina sacra such as ⲓ︤ⲥ︥ (Iēsous) and ⲡ̄ⲛ︤ⲁ︥ (pneuma) are preserved as "Jesus" and pneuma respectively; bracketed lacunae [ ] mark restored or damaged text; angle-brackets ⟨ ⟩ mark editorial supplements; the Coptic ⲧⲙⲛ̄ⲧⲉⲣⲟ (kingdom) is rendered "basileia — reign" maintaining consistency with the Greek convention; ⲡⲓⲱⲧ (Father) and ⲡⲏⲩⲉ (heavens) are rendered straightforwardly. The Greek loanword monachos is preserved untranslated with bracketed gloss "single-ones / solitary-ones" on first occurrence; this term is theologically loaded in Thomas (it appears in Logia 16, 49, 75) and any single English rendering loses semantic range. Eikōn (Genesis 1:26–27 vocabulary) is preserved untranslated with bracketed gloss in Logion 22.61
Excluded from this canon's selection of Thomas (with reasons):
- Logia 1, 4, 10, 12, 13, 15, 18, 19, 23, 25, 27, 28, 30, 32–34, 37, 38, 42, 43, 49–53, 56, 58–62, 67, 69–71, 77, 80–85, 87, 88, 93, 97, 98, 102, 105, 106, 108, 110–112, 114 — distinctively esoteric, body-suspicious, or proto-gnostic sayings (e.g., 50: origins-from-light cosmology with Valentinian "movement and rest"; 56 and 80: world-as-corpse / body-as-corpse encratite framing; 87 and 112: pair-cursed body-soul interdependence; 114: female-must-become-male anthropology); or sayings without clear synoptic, Pauline, or Jewish-wisdom corroboration; or short framing/interpretive sayings whose function is internal to the Thomas collection.
- Logion 7 (the lion) — clear Greco-Roman philosophical parallel (Plato Republic IX 588–589 on the soul as multi-headed beast plus lion plus human); no independent Christian or Jewish attestation for any portion.
Logion 2 — Seeking and finding (cf. P.Oxy 654; cf. Gospel of the Hebrews via Origen)
Jesus said, "Let the seeking-one not cease seeking until he finds; and when he finds, he shall be troubled; and being troubled, he shall marvel; and he shall reign over the All."82
Logion 3 — Kingdom within (cf. Lk 17:21)
Jesus said, "If those leading you say to you, 'Behold, the basileia is in the heaven,' then the birds of the heaven shall precede you. If they say to you, 'It is in the sea,' then the fish shall precede you. But the basileia is within you and outside you. When you know yourselves, then you shall be known, and you shall know that you are the sons of the living Father. But if you do not know yourselves, then you are in poverty, and you are the poverty."83
Logion 5 — Nothing hidden (cf. Mk 4:22, Q; cf. P.Oxy 654)
Jesus said, "Know what is before your face, and what is hidden from you shall be revealed to you. For nothing hidden shall fail to be revealed."
Logion 6 — Don't lie, don't do what you hate
His disciples asked him, they said to him, "Do you wish that we fast? And in what way shall we pray? Shall we give alms? And what shall we observe concerning food?" Jesus said, "Do not lie, and what you hate, do not do; for all things are revealed before the heaven. For there is nothing hidden that shall not become manifest, and there is nothing covered that shall remain without being uncovered."84
Logion 8 — The wise fisherman (cf. Mt 13:47–50)
And he said, "The human is like a wise fisherman who cast his net into the sea; he drew it up out of the sea full of small fish. Among them he found a fine large fish, the wise fisherman; he cast all the small fish back down into the sea; he chose the large fish without difficulty. The one having ears to hear, let him hear."
Logion 9 — The Sower (cf. Mark 4)
Jesus said, "Behold, the sower came-out. He filled his hand, [and] he cast. Some indeed fell upon the road; the birds came, they gathered them. Others fell upon the rock, and they did not strike root down into the earth, and they did not send up an ear to the heaven. And others fell on the thorns; they choked the seed, and the worm ate them. And others fell on the good earth, and it gave good fruit up into the heaven. It came to sixty-fold and a hundred-and-twenty-fold."
Logion 11 — The heavens passing away
Jesus said, "This heaven shall pass away, and the one above it shall pass away. And the dead are not living, and the living shall not die. In the days when you were eating what is dead, you were making it living. When you come to be in the light, what shall you do? On the day when you were one, you became two; but when you have come to be two, what shall you do?"85
Logion 14 — Fasting and what comes out of the mouth (cf. Mk 7:15)
Jesus said to them, "If you fast, you shall beget a hamartia for yourselves; and if you pray, you shall be condemned; and if you give alms, you shall do harm to your spirits. And when you go into any land and walk in the regions, if they receive you, eat what they set before you, heal the sick among them. For what enters your mouth shall not defile you; rather, what comes out of your mouth — that is what shall defile you."86
Logion 16 — Discord, not peace (cf. Q, Lk 12:51–53 // Mt 10:34–36)
Jesus said, "Perhaps the human-beings think that I have come to cast peace upon the kosmos. And they do not know that I have come to cast divisions upon the earth — fire, sword, war. For there shall be five in a house: three shall be against two, and two against three; the father against the son, and the son against the father; and they shall stand as monachos [single-ones]."87
Logion 17 — Eye has not seen (cf. 1 Cor 2:9)
Jesus said, "I shall give you what eye has not seen, and what ear has not heard, and what hand has not touched, and what has not arisen upon the heart of the human-being."88
Logion 20 — The Mustard Seed (cf. Q)
The disciples said to Jesus, "Tell us — the basileia of the heavens, what is it like?" He said to them, "It is like a grain of mustard, smaller than all the seeds; but when it falls onto tilled earth, it sends-out a great branch and becomes shelter for the birds of the heaven."
Logion 21 — Children in the field; the thief; the strong-one
Mariham said to Jesus, "Whom are your disciples like?" He said, "They are like little children dwelling in a field that is not theirs. When the lords of the field come, they shall say, 'Release our field to us.' They strip themselves before them, releasing it to them, giving them their field. Therefore I say: If the master of the house knows that the thief is coming, he shall stay watching before he comes, and shall not let him dig through into his house of his basileia to carry off his goods. But you — be watchful against the kosmos. Gird up your loins with great power, lest the bandits find a way to come to you, since the advantage which you await they shall find. Let there be among you a human-being of understanding. When the fruit ripened, he came quickly, his sickle in his hand, he reaped it. The one having ears to hear, let him hear."89
Logion 22 — Infants nursing; making the two one
Jesus saw infants being-suckled. He said to his disciples, "These infants being-suckled are like those entering the basileia." They said to him, "Then by becoming infants shall we enter the basileia?" Jesus said to them, "When you make the two one, and when you make the inside as the outside, and the outside as the inside, and the upper as the lower; and when you make the male and the female into a single one, so that the male shall not be male nor the female female; when you make eyes in place of an eye, and a hand in place of a hand, and a foot in place of a foot, an eikōn [image] in place of an eikōn — then shall you enter [the basileia]."90
Logion 24 — Light within (cf. Q, Lk 11:33–36 // Mt 5:14–16)
His disciples said, "Show us the place where you are, since it is necessary for us to seek it." He said to them, "The one having ears, let him hear. There is light within a phōteinos [light-one] human-being, and he gives light to all the kosmos. If he does not give light, he is darkness."91
Logion 26 — Speck and Log (cf. Q; cf. P.Oxy 1)
Jesus said, "The speck that is in the eye of your brother — you see [it]; but the beam that is in your eye — you do not see [it]. When you cast-out the beam from your eye, then you shall see-clearly to cast-out the speck from the eye of your brother."
Logion 29 — The wonder of spirit dwelling in flesh (cf. 2 Cor 8:9; cf. Wis 9:15)
Jesus said, "If the sarx came to be on account of pneuma, it is a wonder; but if pneuma came to be on account of the sōma, it is a wonder of wonders. Truly, I marvel at how this great wealth has come to dwell in this poverty."92
Logion 31 — Prophet without honor (cf. Mark 6; cf. P.Oxy 1)
Jesus said, "No prophet is acceptable in his village; no physician heals those who know him."
Logion 36 — Don't worry about clothing (cf. Q, Lk 12:22–31 // Mt 6:25–34; cf. P.Oxy 655)
Jesus said, "Do not worry from morning to evening and from evening to morning what you shall wear."
Logion 39 — Keys of knowledge; snakes and doves (cf. Q, Lk 11:52 // Mt 23:13; Mt 10:16)
Jesus said, "The Pharisees and the scribes have taken the keys of gnōsis [knowledge]; they have hidden them. They have not entered, and those wishing to enter, they have not allowed. But you, become wise as the serpents and innocent as the doves."93
Logion 40 — Grapevine planted apart from the Father (cf. Mt 15:13)
Jesus said, "A grapevine has been planted apart from the Father; and as it is not strengthened, it shall be pulled up by its root and destroyed."
Logion 41 — To the one who has, more shall be given (cf. Mark 4)
Jesus said, "Whoever has [something] in his hand, to him shall be given; and whoever has nothing — even the little he has shall be taken from him."
Logion 44 — Blasphemy against the *pneuma* (cf. Mk 3:28–29; Q)
Jesus said, "Whoever blasphemes against the Father, it shall be released to him; and whoever blasphemes against the Son, it shall be released to him; but whoever blasphemes against the holy pneuma, it shall not be released to him, neither on the earth nor in the heaven."
Logion 45 — Grapes from thornbushes (cf. Q 6 — Lk 6:43–45)
Jesus said, "Grapes are not gathered from thornbushes, nor are figs gathered from camel-thistles; for they do not give fruit. A good human-being brings forth a good thing from his treasure; an evil human-being brings forth evil things from his evil treasure which is in his heart, and he speaks evil things; for out of the abundance of the heart, he brings forth evil things."
Logion 46 — From Adam to John (cf. Q, Lk 7:28 // Mt 11:11)
Jesus said, "From Adam to John the Baptizer, among the begotten of women, no one has been raised who is higher than John the Baptizer, so that his eyes shall not be broken. But I have said: whoever among you shall become as a little-one shall know the basileia, and shall become higher than John."94
Logion 47 — Two masters; new wine in old wineskins (cf. Q and Mark 2)
Jesus said, "It is impossible for a human-being to mount two horses and to draw two bows; and impossible for a slave to serve two masters — or he shall honor the one and shall insult the other. No human-being drinks old wine and immediately desires to drink new wine; and they do not pour new wine into old skins, lest they burst; and they do not pour old wine into new skins, lest it spoil. They do not sew an old patch upon a new garment, since a tear shall come-to-be."
Logion 48 — Two making peace move the mountain (cf. Mk 11:23)
Jesus said, "If two make peace with each other in the same house, they shall say to the mountain, 'Move from here,' and it shall move."
Logion 54 — Beatitude on the poor (cf. Q)
Jesus said, "Blessed are the poor — for yours is the basileia of the heavens."
Logion 55 — Hate father and mother (cf. Q, Lk 14:26–27 // Mt 10:37–38)
Jesus said, "The one not hating his father and his mother shall not be able to become a disciple to me; and the one not hating his brothers and his sisters and bearing his cross in my way [as I do] shall not become worthy of me."95
Logion 57 — Wheat and tares (cf. Matthew 13)
Jesus said, "The basileia of the Father is like a human-being who had [good] seed. His enemy came in the night, he sowed darnel-weed upon the good seed. The man did not allow [the slaves] to pull-up the darnel-weed; he said to them, 'Lest you go to pull-up the darnel-weed, and pull-up the wheat with it.' For on the day of the harvest the darnel-weed shall be manifest; they shall pull it up and shall burn it."
Logion 63 — The rich fool (cf. Luke 12)
Jesus said, "There was a rich human-being who had much money. He said, 'I shall use my money in order that I may sow and may reap and may plant and may fill my storehouses with fruit, that I may not lack anything.' These were the things he was thinking in his heart. And in that night he died. The one who has ears, let him hear."
Logion 64 — Great banquet (cf. Q)
Jesus said, "A human-being had guests; and when he had-prepared the dinner, he sent his slave, that he might invite the guests. He went to the first; he said to him, 'My master invites you.' He said, 'I have money [coming] from some merchants; they are coming to me in the evening; I shall go and give-orders to them. I beg-off from the dinner.' He went to another; he said to him, 'My master has-invited you.' He said to him, 'I have bought a house, and they are asking for me [for] a day; I shall not have leisure.' He came to another; he said to him, 'My master invites you.' He said to him, 'My friend is to be married, and I am the one who shall arrange the dinner; I cannot come. I beg-off from the dinner.' He went to another; he said to him, 'My master invites you.' He said to him, 'I have bought an estate, I am going to receive the rent; I cannot come. I beg-off.' The slave came; he said to his master, 'Those whom you have-invited to the dinner have begged-off.' The master said to his slave, 'Go-out to the roads; whomever you find, bring [them], that they may dine.' Buyers and merchants [shall] not enter into the places of my Father."
Logion 65 — Wicked tenants (cf. Mark 12)
He said, "A good ⟨man⟩ had a vineyard. He gave it to tenants, that they might work it and he might receive its fruit from them. He sent his slave, that the tenants might give him the fruit of the vineyard. They seized his slave; they beat him; a little more and they would have killed him. The slave went; he told his master. His master said, 'Perhaps he did not recognize them.' He sent another slave; the tenants beat that one also. Then the master sent his son. He said, 'Perhaps they shall respect my son.' Those tenants — since they knew that he is the heir of the vineyard — they seized him; they killed him. The one who has ears, let him hear."
Logion 66 — Stone the builders rejected (cf. Mk 12:10–11)
Jesus said, "Show me the stone which the builders rejected; that one is the cornerstone."
Logion 68 — Blessed when hated (cf. Q 3 — Lk 6:22 // Mt 5:11)
Jesus said, "Blessed are you when they hate you and persecute you. And no place shall be found there where they have persecuted you."
Logion 72 — Who made me a divider (cf. Lk 12:13–14)
A human-being said to him, "Tell my brothers to divide my father's possessions with me." He said to him, "O human-being, who has made me a divider?" He turned to his disciples, he said to them, "Surely I am not a divider?"
Logion 73 — Harvest is great (cf. Q — Lk 10:2)
Jesus said, "The harvest indeed is great, but the workers are few. Beg therefore the Kyrios that he send out workers into the harvest."
Logion 74 — Many around the well
He said, "Kyrie, there are many around the drinking-trough, but there is no one in the well."96
Logion 75 — Many at the door; the *monachos* enters
Jesus said, "There are many standing at the door; but the monachos [single-ones] are the ones who shall enter into the place of the wedding."97
Logion 76 — Pearl of great price (cf. Mt 13)
Jesus said, "The basileia of the Father is like a merchant who had a load [of goods]; he found a pearl. That merchant was wise: he sold the load; he bought for himself the single pearl. You also — seek for his treasure that does not perish, that endures, in the place where moth does not enter to eat, nor worm destroy."
Logion 78 — Reed shaken by the wind (cf. Q, Lk 7:24–25 // Mt 11:7–8)
Jesus said, "Why did you go out into the field? To see a reed shaken by the wind, and to see a human-being clothed in soft garments [like your] kings and your great-ones? These are the ones clothed in soft [garments], and they shall not be able to know the truth."
Logion 79 — Blessed womb, blessed hearers (cf. Lk 11:27–28)
A woman from the crowd said to him, "Blessed is the womb that bore you, and the breasts that nourished you." He said to her, "Blessed are those who have heard the logos of the Father and have kept it in truth. For days shall come when you shall say: blessed is the womb that has not conceived, and the breasts that have not given milk."98
Logion 86 — Foxes have holes (cf. Q)

F. The Gospel of John — Selected Pericopes
The Fourth Gospel, c. 90–110 CE, in its final form. The translation philosophy here treats John as a witness whose narrative bones are reliable but whose theological discourses are the evangelist's voice rather than dominical speech. Included accordingly are: early disciple-calling material, the temple cleansing in its Johannine placement, the Samaritan-woman pericope, the feeding of the five thousand, the healing of the man born blind, the raising of Lazarus, the anointing at Bethany, the footwashing, the Johannine passion narrative in its entirety, the resurrection appearances, and the appendix at the Sea of Tiberias. Excluded as Johannine theological composition rather than Jesus-tradition: the Logos prologue (1:1–18); the Nicodemus discourse content (3:1–21); the bread of life discourse (6:25–71); the discrete egō eimi sayings as theological claims (6:35; 8:12; 10:7, 11; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1); the Good Shepherd discourse (10:1–18); the festival discourses (chs. 7–8, much of 10); the farewell discourses and high-priestly prayer (13:18–17:26).
Johannine conventions: the doublet amēn amēn legō hymin is rendered "Amen, amen, I say to you" (preserving the doublet rather than collapsing to "Truly, truly" or "Verily, verily"); Ioudaioi is rendered "Judeans" rather than "the Jews," following the now-standard scholarly consensus that John's term refers most often to the Judean religious authorities or to inhabitants of Judea, not to the ethno-religious group as a whole; the Johannine historic present is preserved as in the synoptics; sēmeion is rendered "sign," ergon "work," martyria "testimony"; paradidōmi "hand over" as in Mark.

Tier 2: Specific Pieces of Reliable Tradition
Material outside the gospels that preserves specific Jesus tradition the source explicitly identifies as received. The unit of inclusion here is the words and deeds the source claims to relay, not the broader theological argument the source is making.
A. The Last Supper Words (1 Corinthians 11:23–26)

Pre-Pauline creedal formula, dated by most scholars to within 2–5 years of the crucifixion; the earliest accessible testimony to the resurrection appearances. Paul's own appearance (15:8, included in section C below with the visionary-encounter caveat) is structurally distinct from the appearances enumerated here.

C. Pauline First-Person Appearance Claims (with caveat)
Paul claims to have seen the risen Jesus, and grounds his apostolic authority on this claim. The category-difference between Paul's encounter and the synoptic-gospel appearances is real and worth preserving in any reading: where the synoptic appearances narrate physical-presence encounter (the risen Jesus eats fish, can be touched, is encountered in shared physical space), Paul himself characterizes his encounter as God revealing the Son en emoi — "in me" (Galatians 1:16) — which is the language of internal vision rather than external physical presence. The texts below are included as testimony to a deed of the risen Jesus (appearing) by a source claiming direct witness, while the visionary character of that appearance is named.

F. The Lord's Prayer and Eucharistic Tradition in the Didache
The Didache (Didachē tōn dōdeka apostolōn) preserved the Lord's Prayer in liturgical context (8:2) and the eucharistic prayers (chs. 9–10). The remainder of the Didache (the Two Ways material, immersion instruction, traveling-prophets material, church order — chs. 1–7 and 11–16) is excluded from this canon as community ethical instruction and church order rather than testimony to specific Jesus words or deeds. Tier 3: Apocryphal Gospels and Fragments — Selected Material
The new criterion opens the door to candidate sources outside the canonical New Testament. Each selection here represents a distinct reliability judgment, made conservatively: material is included where there is genuine scholarly argument for early independent tradition, and excluded where the material is clearly later legend or theological elaboration. Each fragment receives a brief introductory note before its translation describing the source's date, transmission, and the scholarly judgment relevant to its inclusion.
A. Egerton Papyrus 2 — Selected Pericopes
P.Egerton 2 (with the joining fragment P.Köln VI 255) is dated paleographically to the late 2nd century CE; the underlying text is therefore earlier. Bell & Skeat published the editio princeps (Fragments of an Unknown Gospel, 1935); the standard modern edition is in Lührmann, Fragmente apokryph gewordener Evangelien (2000). The fragment preserves four pericopes; three (controversy with religious authorities, healing of a leper, question about tribute) are translated here as candidate testimony with synoptic-style form. The fourth pericope (a fragmentary nature miracle by the Jordan) is excluded as too damaged for reliable translation. The dependence question (early independent tradition vs. 2nd-century composite from the canonical gospels) remains contested; Koester (Ancient Christian Gospels, 1990) and Crossan (Four Other Gospels, 1985) argue for independent early tradition; Wright and Bauckham among others lean toward dependence on the canonical gospels.76

B. Papyrus Oxyrhynchus 1224
P.Oxy 1224 consists of two small papyrus fragments from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE, with the underlying text dated as early as the mid-1st century by some scholars (Crossan) and as late as c. 150 CE by others. The fragments preserve six brief passages of Jesus material, two of which parallel Mark 2:17 and Luke 9:50 / Mark 9:40 with phrasing that shows textual independence from the canonical gospels — i.e., the fragment is not a copy of the synoptic material but appears to draw on parallel tradition.102 Standard editions: Grenfell & Hunt 1914 (The Oxyrhynchus Papyri X, editio princeps); Kraus, Nicklas & Kruger 2009 (Gospel Fragments, OUP). The text is heavily damaged; restorations are conjectural and are marked with brackets following the standard editorial conventions.

C. Papyrus Oxyrhynchus 840 — Temple Purity Controversy
P.Oxy 840 is a small parchment leaf, paleographically dated to the 4th century but transmitting an earlier text (probably 2nd-century). The single pericope preserved is a controversy between Jesus and a chief priest about ritual purity, set in the Temple. Editio princeps: Grenfell & Hunt (1908); the standard modern edition is Kruger, The Gospel of the Savior (2005). The pericope reads as authentic synoptic-style controversy material; it is single-attested and contested, but is included here as candidate testimony.77

D. Papyrus Oxyrhynchus 5575
P.Oxy 5575 is a sayings fragment dated by its editors (Fish, Wallace, Holmes 2023, The Oxyrhynchus Papyri LXXXVII) to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE — making it one of the earliest extant Christian manuscripts. The fragment interweaves material paralleling Matthew 6:25–26, Luke 12:22–24, and Gospel of Thomas Logion 27. The Matthean-parallel sections show evidence of dependence on canonical Matthew (Matthean redactional elements visible) and so are not independent witness; these are marked below with editorial brackets indicating the redundancy. The Thomas-parallel material and the inter-saying linking material may witness a shared sayings-source behind both Thomas and this fragment (Holmes 2023). The fragment is heavily damaged; only the portions where reconstruction is reasonably secure are translated below.106

E. The Gospel of Peter — Passion Material Only
The Akhmim fragment (P.Cair. 10759), discovered in 1886/87 in a monk's grave at Akhmim, preserves a substantial portion of the Gospel of Peter narrating the trial, crucifixion, burial, and resurrection. The work is referenced by Eusebius (Hist. eccl. 6.12) as known and used at Rhossus before being suppressed by Bishop Serapion of Antioch (c. 200 CE) on suspicion of docetism. Crossan (The Cross That Spoke, 1988) has argued for an underlying early "Cross Gospel" preserving independent passion tradition; this judgment is contested. Translated here are the elements paralleling synoptic passion narrative (the trial, crucifixion details, burial, and women at the tomb). The talking-cross episode (vv. 35–42), the giant Jesus motif, and the Roman-soldier resurrection-witness narrative are excluded as later legendary embellishment. Editio princeps: Bouriant (1892), Robinson & James (1892); standard modern edition: Foster, The Gospel of Peter (2010).78

F. Selected Citations from the Jewish-Christian Gospels
The Gospels of the Hebrews, Nazareans, and Ebionites do not survive as continuous texts; they are known only through citations in patristic literature (Origen, Jerome, Epiphanius, Clement of Alexandria, and others). The reconstructed reliability of these citations is contested; included here are three citations of particular interest as candidate Jesus tradition. Standard modern collections: Klijn, Jewish-Christian Gospel Tradition (1992); Klauck, Apocryphal Gospels: An Introduction (2003).
An Agraphon from the Gospel of the Hebrews (Origen)
Origen, Commentary on Matthew 15.14, citing the Gospel of the Hebrews. Greek preserved by Origen. The saying parallels Thomas 2 in similar form, suggesting early independent attestation in two transmission streams.79
"He that wonders shall reign,
and he that has-reigned shall rest."
Resurrection Appearance to James (Jerome)
Jerome, De Viris Illustribus 2, citing the Gospel of the Hebrews. Latin (Jerome's translation); the underlying original would have been Aramaic or Hebrew. This is the only surviving narrative of the appearance to James referenced in 1 Corinthians 15:7. Single late-source attestation; included as candidate testimony.80

And again after a little: "Bring," said the Kyrios, "a table and bread." And immediately added: he took the bread and blessed and broke and gave to James the just, and said to him, "My brother, eat your bread, for the son of the human-being has-risen from those-sleeping."
The Gospel of the Ebionites — Account of Jesus's Baptism (Epiphanius)
Epiphanius, Panarion 30.13.7–8, citing the Gospel of the Ebionites. Greek preserved by Epiphanius. The Ebionite version is doctrinally distinctive (a pluriform heavenly voice; emphasis on the descent of light at the moment of baptism) and may reflect independent baptism tradition shaped by the Ebionite group's adoptionist theology.81
